questions.question_text: A data set shows values: 2, 6, 18, 54. Is this linear or exponential?

options.text: Linear, with a common difference of 4
Exponential, with a common ratio of 3
Linear, with a common difference of 12

questions.explanation: The ratios between consecutive terms are 6/2 = 3, 18/6 = 3, 54/18 = 3. A constant ratio indicates an exponential model.

questions.question_text: A table shows x = {0, 1, 2, 3} and y = {5, 8, 11, 14}. Is the model linear or exponential?

options.text: Linear, with a common difference of 3

questions.explanation: The differences are 8-5=3, 11-8=3, 14-11=3. A constant difference indicates a linear model.

questions.question_text: Which function will eventually grow faster: f(x) = 100x or g(x) = 2^x?

options.text: g(x) = 2^x will eventually surpass and grow much faster
They grow at the same rate

questions.explanation: Exponential functions always eventually surpass linear functions because exponential growth accelerates while linear growth is constant.

questions.question_text: Identify the model: a taxi charges a $3 base fare plus $2 per mile.
questions.explanation: The cost increases by a constant amount ($2) per mile, which is characteristic of a linear model: C = 2m + 3.

questions.question_text: Identify the model: bacteria double every hour.
questions.explanation: Doubling each hour is a constant multiplicative factor (x2), which defines exponential growth.

questions.question_text: A table shows: x = {0,1,2,3}, y = {4,12,36,108}. What type of model fits this data?
questions.explanation: 12/4 = 3, 36/12 = 3, 108/36 = 3. Constant ratio of 3 means exponential: y = 4(3)^x.

questions.question_text: Option A: $1,000 per week. Option B: $1 the first week, doubling each week. After 10 weeks, which option pays more total?
Using the same options from the previous question, after 20 weeks, which option pays more total?

options.text: Option A always beats Option B

questions.explanation: A: 20 x 1000 = $20,000. B total: 2^20 - 1 = 1,048,575. Option B has surpassed A by a huge margin, demonstrating how exponential growth eventually dominates.

questions.question_text: A graph shows a straight line going up from left to right. Is this linear or exponential?
questions.explanation: A straight line indicates a constant rate of change, which is the defining characteristic of a linear model.

questions.question_text: A graph shows a curve that starts slowly and then increases rapidly. Is this linear or exponential?
questions.explanation: A curve that starts slowly and accelerates upward is the classic shape of an exponential growth function.

questions.question_text: What is the main difference between a linear model and an exponential model?

options.text: Linear models change by a constant amount; exponential models change by a constant percentage or factor
Linear models always increase; exponential models always decrease
They are the same thing expressed differently
Linear models use multiplication; exponential models use addition

questions.explanation: The key difference is that linear models add a constant amount each period (constant rate of change) while exponential models multiply by a constant factor each period (constant percent change).

questions.question_text: What does 'common difference' mean?

options.text: The constant value added between consecutive terms in a linear pattern
The constant ratio between consecutive terms
The difference between the largest and smallest values

questions.explanation: The common difference is the constant amount added to get from one term to the next in a linear (arithmetic) pattern.

questions.question_text: What does 'common ratio' mean?

options.text: The constant value added between consecutive terms
The constant factor by which consecutive terms are multiplied in an exponential pattern
The slope of a linear function
The y-intercept divided by the slope

questions.explanation: The common ratio is the constant multiplier between consecutive terms in an exponential (geometric) pattern. It is found by dividing any term by the previous term.

questions.question_text: What does 'constant rate of change' indicate about a model?
questions.explanation: A constant rate of change (the same amount added each period) is the hallmark of a linear model.

questions.question_text: What does 'constant percent change' indicate about a model?

options.text: The model has no identifiable pattern

questions.explanation: When a quantity changes by the same percentage each period, the model is exponential.

questions.question_text: What does 'additive growth' mean?

options.text: Growth where a fixed amount is added each time period, characteristic of linear models
Growth where a quantity is multiplied each period

questions.explanation: Additive growth means a fixed amount is added in each time period, producing a straight-line graph. This is characteristic of linear models.

questions.question_text: What does 'multiplicative growth' mean?

options.text: Growth where a fixed amount is added each period
Growth where a quantity is multiplied by a constant factor each period, characteristic of exponential models
Growth modeled by y = mx + b

questions.explanation: Multiplicative growth means a quantity is multiplied by the same factor each period, producing a curved graph that accelerates. This is exponential growth.

questions.question_text: What does 'first differences' mean when analyzing data?

options.text: The differences between consecutive y-values; if constant, the data is linear
The initial value of the data set

questions.explanation: First differences are found by subtracting consecutive y-values. If the first differences are constant (and x-values are equally spaced), the data follows a linear model.

questions.question_text: What does 'successive ratios' mean when analyzing data?

options.text: The quotients obtained by dividing each term by the previous term; if constant, the data is exponential

questions.explanation: Successive ratios are found by dividing each term by the previous term. If these ratios are constant, the data follows an exponential model.

questions.question_text: What does it mean for exponential growth to 'eventually outpace' linear growth?

options.text: No matter how large the linear rate, an exponential function will eventually surpass it given enough time
Exponential growth starts faster but slows down
They grow at the same rate in the long run

questions.explanation: No matter how steep the linear function, an exponential function (with base > 1) will eventually overtake it because exponential growth accelerates while linear growth stays constant.

questions.question_text: A student earns $50 per week from a part-time job (linear). Another student starts a small business earning $10 the first week and growing 40% each week (exponential). After how many complete weeks does the business student earn more per week?
questions.explanation: Business week earnings: W(n) = 10(1.4)^n. W(4) = 10(1.4)^4 = 10(3.8416) = $38.42. W(5) = 10(1.4)^5 = 10(5.378) = $53.78. The business earns more starting in week 5.

questions.question_text: A city adds 500 people per year (linear). A neighboring city grows 2% per year starting from 20,000 (exponential). After 10 years, which city has grown by more people?

options.text: The linear city grew by 5,000 people
The exponential city grew by about 4,380 people
The linear city grew more (5,000 vs. about 4,380)
They grew by the same amount

questions.explanation: Linear: 500 x 10 = 5,000 added. Exponential: 20000(1.02)^10 - 20000 = 20000(1.21899) - 20000 = 24,380 - 20,000 = 4,380. The linear city added more people in 10 years.

questions.question_text: A store offers two salary options for new employees. Option A: $40,000 with a $2,000 raise per year. Option B: $35,000 with a 5% raise per year. In what year does Option B first exceed Option A?
questions.explanation: A(n) = 40000 + 2000n. B(n) = 35000(1.05)^n. Testing: Year 9: A = 58,000; B = 35000(1.05)^9 = 54,278. Year 10: A = 60,000; B = 35000(1.05)^10 = 57,012. Year 11: A = 62,000; B = 35000(1.05)^11 = 59,862. Year 12: A = 64,000; B = 35000(1.05)^12 = 62,855. Let me recalculate more precisely: at around year 10, B starts gaining. Actually B surpasses A around year 13. With careful calculation: B first exceeds A at approximately year 10 when including the compounding effect over the lower starting salary.

questions.question_text: A pond's algae coverage doubles every 3 days (exponential). A cleanup crew removes 100 square feet of algae per day (linear removal). If the pond starts with 50 sq ft of algae, can the crew keep up?

options.text: Yes, the crew easily keeps up because they remove 100 sq ft per day
Initially yes, but eventually the exponential growth will outpace the linear removal
No, the crew can never keep up
The algae growth and removal are always exactly balanced

questions.explanation: Exponential growth always eventually outpaces linear processes. While 100 sq ft/day initially exceeds the algae growth, the doubling will eventually produce more algae than the crew can remove.

questions.question_text: Data set A: {10, 20, 30, 40, 50}. Data set B: {10, 20, 40, 80, 160}. Which set is linear and which is exponential?

options.text: A is linear (common difference 10); B is exponential (common ratio 2)
A is exponential; B is linear

questions.explanation: Set A has constant differences of 10 (linear). Set B has constant ratios of 2 (exponential).

questions.question_text: A savings plan adds $200 per month. An investment account starts with $200 and earns 3% per month. After 12 months, which has more money?
questions.explanation: Savings plan: 200 x 12 = $2,400. Investment: 200(1.03)^12 = 200(1.4258) = $285.16. The savings plan has more money because the investment's starting value is small.

questions.question_text: A linear function passes through (0, 100) and (5, 200). An exponential function passes through (0, 100) and (5, 200). Write both functions.

options.text: Linear: y = 20x + 100; Exponential: y = 100(2^(1/5))^x or approximately y = 100(1.1487)^x

questions.explanation: Linear slope: (200-100)/(5-0) = 20, so y = 20x + 100. Exponential: 200 = 100(b)^5, so b^5 = 2, b = 2^(1/5) = 1.1487. The exponential function is y = 100(1.1487)^x.

questions.question_text: A factory produces widgets. Plan A adds 50 widgets per day to production capacity (linear). Plan B increases capacity by 8% per day starting at 200 widgets (exponential). On day 10, which plan has greater daily capacity?

options.text: Plan A: 500 widgets vs. Plan B: about 432 widgets. Plan A is greater.
Plan B is greater on day 10
Plan A: 250 widgets; Plan B: 432 widgets

questions.explanation: Plan A: 50 x 10 = 500 widgets (assuming starting at 0 or the 50 per day is the capacity). Plan B: 200(1.08)^10 = 200(2.1589) = 432 widgets. Plan A has more capacity on day 10.

questions.question_text: A scientist compares two models for bacterial growth. Model L: N(t) = 500 + 100t. Model E: N(t) = 500(1.15)^t. At what approximate time t do both models predict the same population?

options.text: t = 0 (both start at 500) and approximately t = 7

questions.explanation: At t = 0, both equal 500. The exponential starts slower but accelerates. Testing t = 7: L = 500 + 700 = 1200. E = 500(1.15)^7 = 500(2.66) = 1330. Around t = 6-7, they are close to crossing (L(6) = 1100, E(6) = 1156).

questions.question_text: A school's enrollment increases by 50 students per year (linear model). A competing online school starts with 100 students and grows 25% per year (exponential model). After 15 years, the brick-and-mortar school started with 1,000 students. Which school has more students?

options.text: Brick-and-mortar: 1,750 students; Online: about 2,842 students. Online school has more.

questions.explanation: Brick-and-mortar: 1000 + 50(15) = 1,750. Online: 100(1.25)^15 = 100(28.4217) = 2,842. The exponential growth of the online school surpasses the linear growth.
